concepts.title: The Bell Labs Wing
concepts.thesis: One New Jersey institution produced more of this archive's source material than any other. Bell Laboratories treated images as signals and signals as images — Julesz's random-dot experiments, Harmon and Knowlton's minimum-information faces, VanDerBeek's poem films, and Freeny and Gabbe's thunderstorm all came from the same buildings, often the same corridors. The wing's throughline: perception itself as an engineering problem, with art arriving as the experiment's most durable output.

members.why: Julesz's random-dot Lincoln — perception as signal processing
Poem Field — VanDerBeek + Knowlton's BEFLIX films
Computer Cubism — Harmon/Knowlton's Lincoln, first pass
Block Portrait — the minimum-information face continued
Knowlton teaching VanDerBeek to program
Information Aesthetics — Bense reads Bell Labs
200 Squares — the picturephone prediction
Freeny & Gabbe's thunderstorm — weather as data film
Denes's computer speech on the DDP-224 — Bell Labs finds its voice

concepts.title: Prediction Fulfilled
concepts.thesis: The archive's signature discovery: NB repeatedly pairs a historical prediction with a contemporary artwork that fulfills it. Harmon designed his 200 squares for picturephones that became LED walls; Csuri foresaw computer-milled sculpture in 1967; Molnár described prompt-based delegation in 1981; Lauckner's 'exotic paint brush' became copy-paste as fine art. The curatorial rule these days established: render from the fulfilling work's substrate, not the historical artifact's.

members.why: 1971 picturephone pixels → 2024 outdoor LED wall
Molnár, 1981: 'predicting 2026' — generative delegation
Csuri's milling-machine prophecy → CNC-carved 'Ridges Over'
Lauckner's 'exotic paint brush' → copy-paste as method

concepts.title: Women Pioneers
concepts.thesis: Bute was bending oscilloscope light into film in the 1930s; Molnár built her machine imaginaire before she had a machine; Sollfrank's generator is still running; Schwartz's records surfaced by accident inside another day's scrape; Freeny narrated her own thunderstorm in 1968 — 'My name is Anne Freeny.' The archive keeps finding that the field's institutional memory forgot its women faster than its machines, and keeps correcting for it.

members.why: Molnár's magic square — the machine imaginaire
Zero 10 — Molnár among the four pioneers
The Diabolical Instrument — Molnár's INA footage
Mary Ellen Bute — abstract film before the computer
Sollfrank's Net Art Generator; Schwartz in the margins
Molnár predicting 2026 in 1981
Anne Freeny — statistician as computer filmmaker

concepts.title: The Newsprint Archive
concepts.thesis: Much of early computer art survives not as artworks but as newspaper coverage of artworks — halftone-grained, deckle-edged, warm with age. These days established the documentation-as-substrate thesis: when the source is a clipping, the render lives on the clipping's paper (palette 8), because the archive doesn't just preserve the work, it interprets the act of preservation.

members.why: Vellum Plotter — the substrate thesis begins
'I was a painter' — Mohr in IBM's pages
Machine-made abstraction on warm newsprint
Bense's theory read through a 1971 clipping
The Ann Arbor News holds up Lauckner's print

concepts.title: Order & Disorder
concepts.thesis: The central drama of the 60s plotter canon — Molnár's 1% of disorder, Nees's tumbling Schotter squares, Mohr's exhaustive rigor — and, since engine v3.1.0, a literal parameter of this archive's own instrument. These are the days where systematic order and its collapse are the subject: magic squares, perfect centrosymmetry, grid subdivision, and a thunderstorm crossing a rain-gauge network as the disorder dial's first assignment.

members.why: Dürer's magic square — order as generative rule
Molnár's systematic displacement
Albers/CLICKBAIT — symmetry 1.0, the archive's maximum order
Nake's grid subdivision — twenty minutes of deliberation
The storm crosses the instrument — first disorder decision, 0.45/linear

concepts.title: The Institutions
concepts.thesis: Movements and societies, not individuals: Cybernetic Serendipity legitimized the field in 1968 London; New Tendencies organized it from Zagreb; the Computer Art Society advocated the 'exotic paint brush'; Art Basel's Zero 10 booth canonized it half a century later. When NB cites an institution rather than an artist, the argument is always legitimation — technology admitted into the arts as serious practice.

members.why: Nove Tendencije — Zagreb organizes the field
Cybernetic Serendipity — ICA London, 1968
Zero 10 — Art Basel canonizes the pioneers
The Computer Art Society's U.S. chairman

concepts.title: The Csuri Room
concepts.thesis: Four visits, four lenses: the figurative source dissolved into plotter accumulation, the underdocumented digitizing process, his place in the machine-abstraction lineage, and finally his own 1967 prophecy of conversational co-authorship — fulfilled by the tools that made these very renders. The archive's deepest single-artist study; the full lineage page holds the detailed argument.

members.why: Bearded Man — the face dissolved into the ring
Point by Point — the process day
Programmed randomness in the 1950–67 lineage
Sinescape — the prophecy in his own words

concepts.title: The Molnár Room
concepts.thesis: The archive's most recurrent single figure, and its conscience. Vera Molnár (1924–2023) built her machine imaginaire — working through algorithmic procedures by hand — before she had a machine to run them on, then spent decades introducing exactly 1% of disorder into otherwise perfect grids because, she said, it made the image come alive. Her 'diabolical instrument' is the labyrinth; her Dürer magic square is order as generative rule; and in a 1981 television interview she described, precisely, the prompt-based delegation that would not arrive for another forty-five years. She is the disorder dial's patron saint and the room the whole archive keeps returning to.

members.why: Instrument Diabolique — Dürer's magic square as generative rule
Labyrinth Protocol — the diabolical instrument's signature
The Diabolical Instrument — her 1981 INA footage
Predicting 2026 — generative delegation described in 1981

concepts.title: The Hockney Triptych
concepts.thesis: Three consecutive days on David Hockney's death, structured as a single argument in three movements: obituary, theory, practice. The archive refused elegiac sentiment and instead made the case Hockney himself made — that a fine-art master picking up a fax machine, a Quantel Paintbox, an iPad is not novelty but continuity, the same interrogation of how we see carried into new instruments. Vivid color gives way to the grey archive of Secret Knowledge gives way to the embodied synthesis of the photographic drawings. The room where the fine-art tradition and the digital tool stop being opposites.

members.why: Sustained Interrogation — the obituary as argument (vivid color)
Secret Knowledge — the theory (grey archive)
Photographic Drawing — the practice (synthesis)

concepts.title: The Mohr Arc
concepts.thesis: A three-day canonization arc built as artwork → mind → machine. Manfred Mohr — a painter who, reading information theory, concluded the computer was the only honest way to make the work he wanted — is approached first through the artworks themselves (the P-050 line distributions, the Hommage), then through his own devastating words in IBM's pages ('works of art are answers to precise questions'), and finally through the CDC 6400 whose circular CRT physically shaped his rotor forms. The archive's clearest demonstration of its hardware-shapes-form thesis: the machine is not neutral, and Mohr knew it.

members.why: Nothing Else — the artworks (P-050, Hommage)
I Was a Painter — the intellectual position (IBM, 1977)
CDC 6400 — the circular-CRT hardware that shaped the form

concepts.title: Haring at the Screen
concepts.thesis: Keith Haring called his 1989 computer work 'primitive high-tech' — and the phrase names the whole room. The subway-chalk sensibility, the bold uninterrupted outline, the compulsion to fill a ground with marks until it is completely full: none of it softened when Haring moved to the Amiga screen. He is the archive's proof that Pop Art and the computer were never opposites — that a graphic vocabulary built for public walls translated to the pixel grid without losing a watt of conviction. Where Mohr reasoned his way to the machine and Molnár theorized it, Haring simply grabbed it and kept drawing.

members.why: Amiga Graffiti — subway walls to the Amiga screen, same conviction
Primitive High-Tech — Haring's own phrase for the 1989 work
Untitled (Robot), 1988 — bold black outline on mustard flat ground
Pop Mosaic — Haring beside Warhol at the same screen
Screen Anthem — flat-color marks on a navy sea

concepts.title: Light as Medium
concepts.thesis: From phosphor to projector: days where emitted light is not how the work is displayed but what the work is made of. The CDC 6400's circular CRT shaped Mohr's compositions; BEFLIX tiles burned on film; Harmon's squares found their destined LED wall; Oursler's eyes breathe on projected spheres. The hardware-shapes-form thesis, luminous edition.

members.why: Amiga Debbie — ProPaint colors on a dark CRT
CDC 6400 — the circular screen shapes the work
Poem Field — CRT tiles as light emission
The electron beam as drawing instrument
The LED wall Harmon predicted
Oursler — video eyes projected onto spheres

concepts.title: The Network
concepts.thesis: Art whose medium is transmission itself: a satellite carrying an image 1000km between Berlin and Paris; a URL that is the artwork; a generator that has been running unattended since 1997; color provenance recorded on a blockchain. Each is the same claim in a different decade — that the signal path, not the endpoint, is where the work lives.

members.why: Une Image Venue d'Ailleurs — telematic transit
jesusswimming.com — the URL as artwork
Sollfrank's generator, still running
CLICKBAIT — the blockchain as color atlas
